Start reading
Product design case study · Second Brain · 2026

How I transformed a nightly stream of vanishing AI session logs into evidence-linked intelligence a team can trust.

When someone works through a hard problem with an AI assistant, they produce real knowledge: a decision, a trade-off, a fix. None of it is saved anywhere a person can find later, so the same problems get solved again and again. Second Brain catches that knowledge the moment it is made and hands it back: to the person who made it, and to the people who need it next.

My role · Product Designer Scope · vision → working product Data → Storage → Intelligence
The problem I designed within The shipped screens
second‑brain · your day, understood
PATTERNYou named constraints up front in 9 of 12 sessionsstrong
DECISION3 architecture calls captured, none written down elsewherenew
RHYTHMYour deep-work window runs 10:30am–2:30pminsight
BLIND SPOTTesting never came up in your prompts this weekwatch
Every insight links back to the exact session it came from. Illustrative mockup, dummy data.
Problem

Developers solve hard problems with AI every day, and the reasoning inside that work, the decisions, constraints, trade-offs and fixes, is never captured. The raw sessions are stored but unread: an honest month-long audit found 2,469 commands and conversations stored and close to 0% of the intelligence inside them ever extracted. Knowledge evaporates nightly, and the same problems get re-solved.

Solution

An intelligence layer over the work a team already does. Sessions are captured with consent, translated into structured insight, and given back twice: a private, evidence-linked mirror for each developer, and aggregate patterns for leadership, separated by a permission boundary that is structural, not a setting. Every claim carries a receipt back to the exact session it came from.

My role

Product designer: I designed the trust architecture, the evidence-link contract, and every surface here, and carried them to a working product; the intelligence methods are built alongside our engineering team, with me as the bridge between engineering realities and user needs.

Impact
  • 100+ developers onboarded in internal rollout, generating the capture layer end to end.
  • A full month of real activity (the audit's 2,469 commands) processed end to end.
  • The trust loop worked: the v1 mirror's lie was caught the same morning it shipped.
  • A silent loss surfaced: the first time there was a place to look, a whole project's history was found already erased by retention.
The complex problemThe baseline pain, shown before it is told

The knowledge is real. Nothing keeps it.

Picture a developer deep in an AI session. Over a day they make dozens of real decisions: why this approach, which constraint mattered, what they tried that failed. It is some of the most valuable thinking the company produces. And when the day ends, it is gone: unread, unsearched, and often literally deleted.

I measured how much we were keeping. It was almost nothing.

Before designing anything, I measured a month of one developer's activity to see how much of what they generated we were actually keeping. The honest result became the entire pitch:

The honest audit, one month of a developer's activity
stored
2,469
commands & conversations captured, raw, untouched
understood
0
pieces of information actually extracted from them
captured
~0%
of the intelligence inside, the most honest number we had

This is what the "stored" knowledge actually looks like

The audit's 2,469 commands were never lost; they were sitting on disk the whole time, in rows shaped like this. Somewhere inside this one is a real architectural decision.

Meanwhile, the only visibility anyone had was usage. The existing tools could say how much we used AI: sessions, tokens, percentages of a limit. None of them could say what we were learning:

A prompt is never just a prompt: inside almost every one is a decision, a constraint, or a piece of reasoning that exists nowhere else. And it is scattered across every tool a developer touches, generated all day, captured by none of them as knowledge:

Claude CodeAI coding sessions
CursorAI editor
Copilotinline AI
Geminiassistant
Shellcommands
GitHubcommits & PRs
Jiratickets

The question that started the project: what if every AI coding session's intelligence our team produced was actually saved?

What losing it costs, in one scene

The names are illustrative; the pattern plays out on every team.

Monday11:00 am
Aarav spends two hours with his AI assistant working out why the payments API is slow. Together they land the fix: cache the response at the API layer, because the legacy database schema can't be touched. He ships it. The decision, and the reason behind it, exist only inside that chat.
Monday6:00 pm
The session ends. That reasoning is now buried in a log nobody will ever open. To the rest of the team, it may as well never have happened.
3 weeks later9:30 am
Priya picks up a ticket on the same API. She has no idea Aarav already solved this. She starts from zero, re-investigating for hours, or worse, "fixing" it by changing the very schema the legacy system depends on.
Without Second Brain

The team pays for the same lesson twice. Aarav's hard-won reasoning is gone, and Priya is about to spend her own, or reintroduce the exact bug that constraint was there to prevent.

With Second Brain

The moment Aarav's session ends, the decision is captured. "cache at the API layer; don't touch the legacy schema", linked to his real prompt. When Priya opens the ticket, the answer is already waiting, with the evidence behind it. Two hours saved, and the decision is now the team's memory, not one person's.

Five frustrations, named

I gathered these from my own work and my teammates': the recurring pains that proved this was a real problem, not a nice-to-have. Every design choice later traces back to one of them.

01

The best thinking is write-only.

Decisions and reasoning are made constantly inside sessions, then never resurface. The same questions get re-answered and the same mistakes re-made, quarter after quarter.

02

The data literally disappears.

AI tools clean up after themselves. Whole projects' worth of history vanish on a retention timer. "there's nothing left for the app to show."

03

The person who made it has no mirror.

A developer can't see their own patterns, when they do their best work, where they get stuck, what they've learned. There is no reflection to look into.

04

Leaders reach for the wrong numbers.

Without a real signal, "productivity" collapses into token counts and prompt tallies. This is now a documented, industry-wide enterprise challenge, not a hunch: large engineering orgs publicly dismantled internal usage leaderboards in 2026 after the numbers got gamed, and AI budgets burned with no clear link to shipped output. Usage dashboards measure motion; Second Brain measures the judgment inside the motion, the outcome layer the industry publicly pivoted toward.

05

Even when it's stored, it's unreadable.

Raw session data is an unstructured wall of text. Stored is not the same as understood, and nobody was ever going to read it by hand.

!

The stakes

Left unsolved, the single most valuable by-product of an AI-native team, its accumulated reasoning, stays write-only forever. That was the problem worth designing around.

The constraints I designed within

Constraints are design material, and this product had ten hard ones in three families. Every screen in the execution section names the constraint that shaped it.

Technical
The material fights back.
  • T1Raw machine logs. Sessions land as machine-shaped rows; storing them is not understanding them.
  • T2Local-first data. The records live on the developer's own machine; capture is a companion they choose to install, and nothing leaves the machine without consent.
  • T3Data that deletes itself. The tool's own retention quietly erases history.
  • T4No ground-truth labels. Nothing in the stream says which conclusions are true, so every insight must carry its own proof.
Business
The economics have to close.
  • B1Token spend. Deriving intelligence costs model calls; every insight has to be worth its compute.
  • B2One designer, crossing into engineering. Vision, design, and build had to fit one person's capacity on a roughly three-month runway.
  • B3Proven on real work. The product had to earn trust on the team's own live sessions, not a curated demo corpus.
Ethical
The product reads people's work.
  • E1The surveillance fear. A mirror becomes a monitor the moment it stops serving the person it watches.
  • E2Aggregates-only leadership. Raw session text is never visible above the developer themselves.
  • E3Consent that stays revocable. Opt-in per person, per tool; disabling is not retroactive punishment; secrets are stripped before anything is surfaced.

For a product that reads people's work, the consent and safety surface is the design problem. These are not afterthoughts; they are the brief.

Research & empathyThe working record is the user research, verbatim

The research was already written. I had to learn to read it.

This project's user research is unusual, and deliberately so: the quotes below are taken verbatim from the real working record, typos preserved, and they are labeled as the project's real user research. The people quoted are the actual users I designed for, caught in the exact moment of needing the product.

the working record · the thesis, stated before any design existed

"the main idea of second brain is that currently there is no visibility of data for the user, but that unstructured data has very rich data that needs to be extracted properly."

the working record · opening a project whose history retention had already deleted

"there's nothing left for the app to show."

the working record · the brief that defined the mirror's voice

"You are the developer's daily coach — a calm, observational mirror… for tired engineers who have 30 seconds."

the working record · on finding hidden fields riding along in an API payload

"a very big security lapse"

Four moments, four requirements. Make the invisible data visible (the thesis). Never pretend an absence is not real (the loss). Speak to the developer calmly and briefly (the voice). And treat any possible exposure as a structural failure, not a bug (the lapse). Everything that follows is an answer to one of these.

The two people every decision had to serve

The working record kept splitting "the user" in two, and that split is the design's real tension: the developer I design for, and the leader I design around. Each card ends with the promise the product has to keep.

Archetype 01 · the protected party
The Privacy-Wary Developer

What they wantTheir work to stay theirs: a useful reflection of their own patterns, without exposure to anyone above them.

What they fearA mirror that is secretly a monitor; their name on a leaderboard; a transcript read in a meeting they are not in.

So the design mustKeep raw text at the person level, make "no drill-down" structural rather than a permission flag, and keep consent per tool and revocable. The subject sees everything about themselves before anyone above sees anything at all.

Archetype 02 · the accountable party
The Board-Facing CTO

What they wantHonest patterns: where the team is strong, where it is stuck, what it collectively knows, in numbers that can be defended upward.

What they fearActing on vanity metrics that get gamed, and rolling out a tool their own developers refuse to accept.

So the design mustServe aggregates that stay tied to evidence, admit uncertainty instead of crying wolf, and draw the privacy boundary so clearly the CTO can show it to the team as a guarantee, not a policy.

Archetype 03 · the same developer, another day
The Mirror-Wanting Developer

The privacy-wary developer and this one are often the same person in different moods: they genuinely want to see their own patterns, when their best work happens, where they get stuck, what they have learned. What they will not accept is a mirror that flatters or lies. For them, the design must show its receipts and admit what it does not know.

The questions the CTO archetype actually asks

These are the questions a leader cannot get a straight answer to today, and how captured knowledge answers them from the work itself, without crossing the privacy boundary.

"Is the payments project actually on track?"
TodaySchedule a sync, get a filtered, self-reported update, and still not be sure. It takes days to feel the real ground truth.
With Second BrainFuture work, designed not shipped: see that it quietly absorbed 4 scope changes this sprint, roughly 12 developer-days of rework, read straight from the work. No meeting needed.
"If one person left tomorrow, what would break?"
TodayGuess from job titles, or run a manual audit that takes days and is usually wrong.
With Second BrainFuture work, designed not shipped: a map built from what people actually do shows one engineer is the only person who understands a critical service, a risk you can act on before they hand in their notice.
"What did we decide three weeks ago, and why?"
TodayNobody wrote it down. The reasoning lived in a chat that has since been deleted.
With Second BrainA decision record that writes itself from the work, every choice, with the constraint that drove it.

One of these is substantiable today: the decision record. The Aarav-style decisions in this very case study, "cache at the API layer; don't touch the legacy schema", were recovered from real session history by exactly this mechanism; the harder leadership claims (rework costing, bus-factor maps) remain future work and are labeled as such.

Ideation & IAThe mental model, the trust architecture, one flow, and the skeleton

Privacy is the shape of the product. Here is the shape.

Before any screen, three structural decisions: a mental model simple enough to say in one breath, an information architecture where the privacy boundary is a wall rather than a checkbox, and one named user journey the whole product exists to serve.

The mental model: three moves, no new work

1

The raw material

The work a team already does with its AI tools. No new busywork, no forms to fill in, the everyday exhaust becomes the input.

2

Made sense of

The unstructured mess is turned into structured, traceable understanding, and every insight stays tied to the real moment it came from.

3

Given back, per person

A developer gets a private mirror of their own work; a leader gets honest patterns in aggregate, never a raw look over anyone's shoulder.

Eight questions the data must answer

To keep "intelligence" from becoming a vanity dashboard, I framed everything Second Brain derives as a question, because a question is falsifiable and a dashboard tile is not. Every surface in the product has to answer one of these, or it does not earn its place:

01
Developer behaviorWho is this developer, really, by how they work, not their job title?
02
Problems & solutionsWhat problems does this team face, and what knowledge is being generated solving them?
03
Decisions & knowledgeWhat has been decided, why, and where does that reasoning live now?
04
Time & workflowWhen does good work happen, and what does its rhythm reveal?
05
Project & deliveryWhat is each project's true health and trajectory?
06
The codebaseWhat does the codebase actually feel like to work in?
07
Collective knowledgeWhat does the team collectively know, and where are the gaps?
08
CollaborationHow do people actually work together?

The two commitments the architecture encodes

Commitment 1
Every insight is evidence-linked.

Nothing Second Brain says about you is a floating judgment. It always points back to the actual session it came from, so you can check it yourself. A claim you can't verify is a claim you won't trust, so there are none. And when the system isn't sure, it says so: a low-confidence insight renders as a question, not a verdict.

Commitment 2
Privacy is the shape of the product, not a setting.

A person sees their own detail. Leadership sees honest patterns in aggregate, never a raw trace, never a look over a shoulder. This boundary isn't a toggle you can flip; it's built into what each role can ever see.

The trust architecture

This is the diagram the whole product hangs from. Data flows from the developer's private mirror, full detail and evidence links, across a hard permission boundary, into the leadership aggregate view. The boundary is not a role check a future admin could loosen; it is drawn as a wall because in the product it behaves like one: identity and raw text structurally cannot cross it. That makes the system secure by structure, and it is why the same architecture scales from one developer to a whole org without renegotiating trust at each step. It is also what keeps the platform agent-native: an autonomous agent's session enters the same capture, crosses the same boundary, and carries the same receipts as a human one.

The developer's private mirror

full detail · visible only to the person it is about
Raw sessions, captured locally with per-tool consent (T2 · E3)
Evidence-linked insights: every claim keeps its receipt (T4)
Verbatim quotes from the developer's own prompts
The correction loop: "this is wrong" feeds back into the system
the permission boundary

The leadership aggregate view

patterns only · identity stripped · no drill-down exists (E2)
Team patterns: where the team is strong, where it is stuck
Counts and trends, never a transcript
Collective knowledge: what the org keeps when people move on
Open a raw session: this capability does not exist at this level
crosses the wallaggregates · patterns · counts, identity stripped never crossesraw text · verbatim prompts · anything with a name on it

The same wall explains the leader's screen in the execution section: its greyed button is not "disabled", the drill-down capability is absent by structure. What developers were promised is exactly what the architecture can physically deliver.

One journey, end to end: trusting the daily mirror

The flow the whole product optimizes is not "view a dashboard". It is the trust loop: receive, expand, verify, correct. A mirror that cannot be corrected is a verdict, and verdicts get ignored.

The developer receives their daily mirrora handful of small claims about yesterday
→
Expands one insightthe claim opens in place, receipt attached
→
Verifies the evidence linktheir own words, the exact source session
→
Does the receipt hold up?
→
yes · the claim is believed, trust compounds no · one tap: "this is wrong", the correction is recorded

The unhappy path is a first-class path: the fastest way to earn trust is to make being wrong cheap to prove.

The mirror home, deconstructed

The deconstruction is done on the shipped screen itself: six structural decisions, each marked where it lives on the real mirror home. The numbered notes explain why elements are grouped and ordered the way they are.

1 2 3 4 5 6
  1. 1Orientation before judgment. The day, named in one line, before any claim about it. You always know what is being mirrored.
  2. 2A ranked stack, one line each. Insights order by attention-worthiness, and each collapsed row is just tag, claim, level: scannable without being read, because the tags and levels carry the meaning.
  3. 3Evidence expands in place, directly under the claim it supports. Verification never navigates away; any distance between a claim and its proof is where doubt lives.
  4. 4The receipt is source-first: the session chip and the developer's verbatim words, never a paraphrase. The proof arrives in your own voice.
  5. 5Exactly two actions: open the session, or say this is wrong. Correction sits beside confirmation as an equal, one-tap citizen (the flow above).
  6. 6The mirror closes by looking forward: a small daily-actions strip, reflection first, then one next step. Coaching, not grading.
Prototyping & usability testingThe learning that shaped every surface

Nobody believes a mirror they can't check.

The prototypes produced plenty of plausible insights. What separated a polite nod from a changed behavior was never how smart the claim sounded; it was whether the reader could check it. The biggest win of the whole build is exactly that: every insight links to the actual sessions it came from, and clicking the link drops you into the real conversation. Evidence is what turned the mirror from a novelty into something people act on.

The same insight, with and without its evidence

The insight, alone
PATTERNYou've solved this kind of problem before; the approach that worked for you then would work here too.strong

Plausible, possibly even right, and completely unverifiable. Which problem? When? Says who? There is nothing to click and nothing to check, so the claim just floats. The reader nods and changes nothing, and every unverifiable claim after this one costs the mirror a little more credibility.

The same insight, carrying its receipt
PATTERNYou've seen this before: when the payments API stalled, caching at the API layer worked, without touching the legacy schema. The same pattern fits this ticket.strong
⛓ evidence · why the mirror believes this
session · acme-payments · Mon 11:02turn 14 of 31
"try caching the response at the API layer instead of the db level. we can't change the db schema, the legacy system depends on it"
Open the sessionThis is wrong

One click on the session chip and the reader lands inside the exact conversation the claim came from, their own words, at the moment it happened. The insight stops being an opinion and becomes a finding with an address: verify it in seconds, act on it with confidence, or flag it as wrong on the spot.

That pair is the whole learning. The claim on both sides is identical; only the right side gets believed, and only the right side changes what someone does next. Linking every insight to its source sessions, and redirecting the reader straight into them on click, is the single biggest reason the mirror earns trust. The anatomy below dissects exactly this card.

second-brain · session trace · acme-payments▶ open the clickable mockup
Where the receipt leads. Tapping Open the session on any insight drops the reader into the real trace, every turn kept verbatim, with the exact cited turn highlighted. The insight cited caching at the API layer; here is turn 6 of 8, in the developer's own words, inside the debugging session it came from. A claim you can walk back to its source is a claim you can trust.

The morning that made evidence non-negotiable

v1 · confident claims, no receipts

The lesson arrived the hard way. v1 of the mirror shipped fast, looked finished, and reported nonsense: on the first day after install it aggregated the entire stored history, every session the machine had ever captured, and presented the total as a single day, 164 sessions, ~49,793 hours, 11M tokens, in a first-person voice, with not one claim a human could check.

the working record · the morning v1 shipped

"164 sessions in a single day is not justifyable."

A wrong number with no receipt looks identical to a right one. The claim was not just wrong, it was uncatchable; a receipt would have exposed it in one tap. That morning, evidence-linking stopped being a nice idea and became the product's first commitment.

v2 · every claim carries its receipt

v2 rebuilt around the receipt: the voice moved to second person, because a mirror that says "I" is pretending to be you; the claims got smaller, because small claims can be checked; and every claim gained its evidence link, because the only person who can catch a lie about your day is you. Tap the claim, see the receipt. (high-fidelity mockup, dummy data)

Anatomy of the insight card

The insight card is the atom the whole product is built from, and every part of it exists because of the learning above. Here it is rebuilt at component scale, with the five load-bearing parts numbered:

DECISION 1 You decided to cache at the API layer; the legacy schema stays untouched. 2new
⛓ evidence · why the mirror believes this
3session · acme-payments · Mon 11:02turn 14 of 31
4 "try caching the response at the API layer instead of the db level. we can't change the db schema, the legacy system depends on it"
5Open the sessionThis is wrong
  1. 1The claim. Second person, one sentence, small enough to be checkable. The card never says more than its receipt can prove.
  2. 2The confidence treatment. A confident insight is a declarative sentence with a level chip. A low-confidence one changes voice entirely: it renders as a question ("this might be a pattern, worth a look?"), never a verdict.
  3. 3The session chip. The receipt's address: which session, which project, which moment. Provenance is a visible part of the claim, not metadata.
  4. 4The verbatim receipt. Your own words, quoted exactly, typos and all. A paraphrase could hide an error; a quote cannot.
  5. 5The correction. "This is wrong" sits beside "Open the session" as an equal action. Making the mirror cheap to correct is what makes it worth believing, and it is how v1's lie would have been caught in one tap.

This is how the receipt builds trust: the card puts the claim and the means of falsifying it in the same visual unit. You never have to take the mirror's word for anything, and that is exactly why people do.

The surface evolved as much as the logic

The interaction model iterated above; the surface iterated with it. These are real earlier prototypes from the working record, shown with mock data.

Iteration 1 · the daily digest ideaThe earliest mirror concept. “yesterday was a heavy debugging day” with a snapshot strip and one suggested task. The bones of the coach are already here; the trust machinery isn’t.▶ open this iteration
Iteration 2 · the memory-evolution simulationA week of memory updates made visible, what the pipeline “learned tonight.” This is where evidence-linking and the day-by-day rhythm were worked out, in the serif-and-cream era of the design.▶ open this iteration
Final · the shipped coach homeThe AI-Coach home in the zinc system: severity-grouped insights, evidence expansion, daily actions. Same bones as iteration 1, with everything the failures taught layered in.▶ open this iteration

The visual journey mirrors the product one: from a cream, serif concept that looked like a journal to a working tool that behaves like one, the aesthetic converged once the trust mechanics did.

Final executionEach screen tied back to the constraint that shaped it

Three screens, and the constraints that shaped them.

Claims about trust are cheap, so here are the surfaces themselves, each tied back to a named constraint from the problem section. Every screen below is a real, fully clickable high-fidelity mockup with dummy data: navigation, filters, and evidence that expands to the real session. Use the ▶ button on any frame to open it live. (The production surfaces, with real data, are walked through in person.)

second-brain · daily insights · the coach home▶ open the clickable mockup
Screen 1 · The developer's mirror, receipt open. The design argument is the expanded card: an insight is a claim plus its evidence, and its confidence: the source session, your verbatim words, and a one-tap way to say "this is wrong." Nothing floats. This exists because of T4 and E1: with no ground-truth labels in the data, the proof has to travel with the claim, and a mirror that shows its receipts is the structural opposite of a monitor.
second-brain · team patterns▶ open the clickable mockup
Screen 2 · The leader's view, privacy as structure. The important design decision is what's missing: there is no path from a pattern to a person's transcript. The greyed button isn't disabled by permission; the capability doesn't exist at this level. This exists because of E2: aggregates-only leadership is the wall from the trust architecture, rendered. It is what let developers say yes.
second-brain · sessions · project: ostrich▶ open the clickable mockup
Screen 3 · The empty state that tells the truth. This exact loss, opening a project and finding retention had erased it, is the moment that started the product (more in the retrospective). This exists because of T3: when data deletes itself, absence is information, and a mirror that papered over the gap would be lying by omission.

The component system, at kit scale

Enterprise surfaces live or die on vocabulary discipline. The mirror's entire language is four tags, four levels, and three card states; every screen above is assembled from these and nothing else.

The insight card's three states

Resting · one quiet line
PATTERNYou named constraints up front in 9 of 12 sessionsstrong▾

One quiet line per claim; nothing shouts.

Expanded · the receipt open
DECISIONYou decided to cache at the API layernew▴
⛓ evidence
session · acme-payments · Mon 11:02
Open the sessionThis is wrong

Claim and proof in one visual unit; correction one tap away.

Uncertain · a question, not a verdict
PATTERNthis might be a pattern, worth a look?watch▾

Below full confidence the voice changes: the mirror asks instead of asserting.

The tag and level vocabulary

TokenRenderedWhat it marksType & color logic
Tag · PATTERNPATTERNA behavior seen across sessionsTags are structure, not judgment, so they carry no color: mono, 9.5px, uppercase, wide tracking, muted. They tell you what kind of claim you are about to read.
Tag · DECISIONDECISIONA choice made in the work, and why
Tag · RHYTHMRHYTHMWhen and how the work flows
Tag · BLIND SPOTBLIND SPOTWhat never shows up in the work
Level · strongstrongConfirmed across many sessionsLevels are judgment, so they do carry color, one semantic hue each, used identically everywhere: green = held, blue = first seen, amber = watch, purple = observation. 10.5px, semibold, pill.
Level · newnewFirst time the mirror has seen it
Level · watchwatchWorth your attention, not an alarm
Level · insightinsightAn observation, nothing to act on
Typography scale
Three sizes, three jobsClaims 14px medium; receipts 12.5px mono (verbatim text is always mono, it signals "quoted, not written"); tags and chips 9.5–10.5px. Nothing else exists.
Spacing logic
Density without noiseCard rows 14×18px padding; the receipt insets 16×18px behind a dashed top border, so evidence reads as "inside" its claim; chips keep 8–10px gaps.
Color logic
Color is meaning, only meaningFour semantic hues, each reserved for one level of judgment. Structure stays monochrome, so the levels are the only things that pop.
RetrospectiveEach lesson anchored to a real moment in the working record

What this actually taught me.

Two kinds of lessons came out of this project, and neither is a principle I started with. Both were forced on me by specific moments in the working record.

What I learned about ethical design

1
Trust broke first from the insideThe sharpest privacy lesson didn't come from a user complaining, it came from catching the product itself over-sharing. Early on I found data that was supposed to be hidden quietly riding along in what the interface fetched: invisible on screen, right there underneath. Nobody had been hurt, but I had built the exact thing I was promising not to be. That's when "privacy as a setting" died and "privacy as structure" (Screen 2, no drill-down exists) replaced it. If exposure is possible, someday it happens; the only honest fix is to make it impossible.
2
The subject comes first, alwaysThe surveillance fear is not answered by a privacy policy; it is answered by disclosure order. The person an insight is about sees it first, in full, with its evidence; anyone above them sees only what aggregates can carry. The mirror speaks to you, never as you, and never says anything about you it cannot show you the receipt for. A product that reads people's work earns its existence structurally or not at all.

What I learned about system architecture

3
Evidence-linking is the product's epistemologyv1 of the mirror looked finished and reported a day that never happened. 164 sessions, fifty thousand hours. I had spent my effort on how it read and none on whether it could be checked. The fix reordered my priorities permanently: a reflective product must be true, then checkable, then pleasant, in that order. The receipt stopped being a feature and became the architecture: every claim in the system, at every level, is only as strong as its link back to the work.
4
Absence is a signal the system must carryThe project didn't start with a vision deck. It started the day I went looking for my own past work, a project I'd spent weeks inside, and found the tool's cleanup had already deleted every trace of it. Nothing left to search, nothing to show. That loss became a design rule: never paper over an absence. Missing data is information, and Screen 3 keeps that moment as a deliberate product surface instead of hiding it behind a friendly illustration.

The through-line

01

The valuable thing was hiding in plain sight, the exhaust of everyday work, not some new data we had to go collect.

02

A tool people trust has to be true before it's pretty, and it has to show its evidence or nobody believes the conclusion.

03

Privacy couldn't be a feature bolted on at the end, it had to be the shape of the thing from the first sketch.

Where it stands, honestly

It runs on real machines, on real work. Capture has run on my machine and my teammates'; a full month of one developer's activity (the 2,469 commands from the audit) has been through the pipeline end to end and come out as structured, checkable insights.
The trust loop has caught a real lie. The clearest evidence the evidence-link design works is the failure in the testing section: v1's wrong mirror was spotted the same morning it appeared, because a human could check the claim, and it forced the two rules every surface now follows.
It surfaced a loss nobody could see. The first time there was a place to look, it revealed that an entire project's history had already been silently deleted by retention; the empty state in Screen 3 is that real moment, not a hypothetical.
A flagship surface shipped. The first full product surface, a viewer that makes an invisible, autonomous process legible, is built and in use. It gets its own case study, linked below.
Honest limits. Capture is shipped and at real scale; insight is in active development, and the two must not be conflated, so this page never claims the 100+ use the mirror yet. Second Brain is in internal rollout: 100+ developers onboarded and generating the capture layer end to end, with feedback actively shaping the product (B3). The intelligence layer, the mirror and its insights, is being derived against that live data now (B1); its trust model has been validated with early users, not yet in a structured study across the full cohort. That study, with its metric named up front, is the next milestone: how often a wrong insight is corrected via its evidence-link rather than ignored.
✎
What was mine, and what wasn't I architected the interaction models and defined the system constraints: the concept, the problem framing, the trust architecture, and every surface shown here are mine, and the execution ran inside guardrails I designed. It was one person carrying design across into engineering (constraint B2: one designer, crossing into engineering), working as the bridge between engineering realities and user needs; the intelligence methods are built with our engineering team, and the derivation work is ongoing. Second Brain is in the current internal rollout.

The trilogy

Second Brain's bet is that a team's invisible, everyday intelligence is worth capturing, and can be made trustworthy enough that people are glad it was. This chapter made the case for the vision; two siblings tell the rest.

→
Next case study · The Session Viewer The first surface that proved the thesis: a tool that turns the black box of autonomous AI work into a story any teammate can replay. The trilogy's interaction deep-dive.
↗
Also in the trilogy · The Leadership Layer What happens on the far side of the permission boundary: composed, confidence-weighted signals a leader can act on, built only from the aggregates this case study drew the wall around.
◎
A note on the evidence The quotes, numbers, and failure moments in this case study are taken from the project's real working record, including the unflattering ones. The screens are faithful recreations with dummy data; the production surfaces are walked through live. Where something isn't measured yet, the text says so instead of rounding up.
SBSecond Brain · a case study

Making a team's invisible, everyday intelligence visible, and giving it back as something a person and an organization can actually trust.

Second Brain, product design case study · 2026 · a work in progress. All quotes are verbatim from the working record, typos preserved; they are this project's real user research. Tool logos are the property of their respective owners, used here to illustrate the everyday tools a developer's work spans. The engineering behind the product is intentionally omitted; it will be a separate technical write-up.
